mn148:1.2: At one time the Bhagavā was dwelling at Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn148:1.3: There the Bhagavā addressed the bhikkhus:
mn148:1.5: “*Bhante*,” those bhikkhus replied to the Bhagavā.
mn148:10.1: ‘The eye is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.11: ‘*Rūpas* are self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.12: Thus the eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:10.13: ‘Eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.14: The arising and passing away of eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇassa*] are discerned.
mn148:10.15: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:10.17: ‘Eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.18: Thus the eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*], eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:10.19: ‘Eye-contact is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.2: The arising and passing away of the eye are discerned.
mn148:10.20: The arising and passing away of eye-contact are discerned.
mn148:10.21: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:10.23: ‘Eye-contact is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.24: Thus the eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*], eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], eye-contact is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:10.25: ‘*Vedanā* is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.26: The arising and passing away of *vedanā* are discerned.
mn148:10.27: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:10.29: ‘*Vedanā* is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.3: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:10.30: Thus the eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*], eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], eye-contact is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:10.31: ‘*Taṇhā* is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.32: The arising and passing away of *taṇhā* are discerned.
mn148:10.33: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:10.35: ‘*Taṇhā* is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.36: Thus the eye is not-self [*anattā*], *rūpas* are not-self [*anattā*], eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], eye-contact is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*], *taṇhā* is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:10.5: ‘The eye is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:10.7: ‘*Rūpas* are self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:10.8: The arising and passing away of *rūpas* are discerned.
mn148:10.9: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:11.1: ‘The ear is self’—anyone who would say that …
mn148:11.2: ‘The nose is self’—anyone who would say that …
mn148:11.3: ‘The tongue is self’—anyone who would say that …
mn148:11.4: ‘The body is self’—anyone who would say that …
mn148:11.5: ‘The mind is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:11.6: The arising and passing away of the mind are discerned.
mn148:11.7: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:11.9: ‘The mind is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:12.1: ‘*Dhammas* are self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:12.2: The arising and passing away of *dhammas* are discerned.
mn148:12.3: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:12.5: ‘*Dhammas* are self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:12.6: Thus the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:13.1: ‘Mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:13.2: The arising and passing away of mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇassa*] are discerned.
mn148:13.3: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:13.5: ‘Mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:13.6: Thus the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*], mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:14.1: ‘Mind-contact is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:14.2: The arising and passing away of mind-contact are discerned.
mn148:14.3: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:14.5: ‘Mind-contact is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:14.6: Thus the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*], mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], mind-contact is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:15.1: ‘*Vedanā* is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:15.11: ‘*Taṇhā* is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:15.12: Thus the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*], mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], mind-contact is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*], *taṇhā* is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:15.2: The arising and passing away of *vedanā* are discerned.
mn148:15.3: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:15.5: ‘*Vedanā* is self’—anyone who would say that.
mn148:15.6: Thus the mind is not-self [*anattā*], *dhammas* are not-self [*anattā*], mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is not-self [*anattā*], mind-contact is not-self [*anattā*], *vedanā* is not-self [*anattā*].
mn148:15.7: ‘*Taṇhā* is self’—anyone who would say that, that is not tenable.
mn148:15.8: The arising and passing away of *taṇhā* are discerned.
mn148:15.9: But when the arising and passing away of something are discerned, it follows for him: ‘My self arises and passes away.’
mn148:16.1: This, bhikkhus, is the practice leading to the arising of self-identification—
mn148:16.2: one regards the eye thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.1: one regards *rūpas* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.10: one regards the mind thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’; one regards *dhammas* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’; one regards mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’; one regards mind-contact thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’; one regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’; one regards *taṇhā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
mn148:17-21.2: one regards eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.3: one regards eye-contact thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.4: one regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.5: one regards *taṇhā* thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’;
mn148:17-21.6: one regards the ear thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’ …
mn148:17-21.7: one regards the nose thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’ …
mn148:17-21.8: one regards the tongue thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’ …
mn148:17-21.9: one regards the body thus: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self’ …
mn148:2.1: “Bhikkhus, I will teach you a *dhamma* that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-worded; I will make known the holy life that is complete, wholly fulfilled, and perfectly pure, namely—
mn148:2.3: Listen to that, attend to it carefully, I will speak.”
mn148:2.4: “Yes, bhante,” those bhikkhus replied to the Bhagavā.
mn148:22.1: This, bhikkhus, is the practice leading to the cessation of self-identification—
mn148:22.2: one regards the eye thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.1: One regards *rūpas* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.10: one regards the mind thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.11: One regards *dhammas* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.12: One regards mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.13: One regards mind-contact thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.14: One regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.15: One regards *taṇhā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.2: One regards eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.3: One regards eye-contact thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.4: One regards *vedanā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.5: One regards *taṇhā* thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
mn148:23-27.6: One regards the ear thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’ …
mn148:23-27.7: one regards the nose thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’ …
mn148:23-27.8: one regards the tongue thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’ …
mn148:23-27.9: one regards the body thus: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’ …
mn148:28.1: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition there arises a felt experience of *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn148:28.2: When touched by pleasant *vedanā*, he delights in it, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn148:28.3: The underlying tendency to passion lies within him.
mn148:28.4: When touched by painful *vedanā*, he sorrows, grieves, laments, beats his breast, and becomes distraught.
mn148:28.5: The underlying tendency to aversion lies within him.
mn148:28.6: When touched by neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he does not understand as it truly is the arising and vanishing of that *vedanā*, its gratification, danger, and escape.
mn148:28.7: The underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] lies within him.
mn148:28.8: That one, bhikkhus, without abandoning the underlying tendency to passion in regard to pleasant *vedanā*, without dispelling the underlying tendency to aversion in regard to painful *vedanā*, without uprooting the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] in regard to neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, without abandoning blindness [*avijjā*] and giving rise to knowledge, would here and now bring dukkha to an end—this is impossible.
mn148:29-33.1: Dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:29-33.10: When touched by neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he does not understand as it truly is the arising and vanishing of that *vedanā*, its gratification, danger, and escape.
mn148:29-33.11: The underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] lies within him.
mn148:29-33.12: That one, bhikkhus, without abandoning the underlying tendency to passion in regard to pleasant *vedanā*, without dispelling the underlying tendency to aversion in regard to painful *vedanā*, without uprooting the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] in regard to neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, without abandoning blindness [*avijjā*] and giving rise to knowledge, would here and now bring dukkha to an end—this is impossible.
mn148:29-33.2: dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:29-33.3: dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:29-33.4: dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:29-33.5: dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition there arises a felt experience of *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn148:29-33.6: When touched by pleasant *vedanā*, he delights in it, welcomes it, and remains holding to it.
mn148:29-33.7: The underlying tendency to passion lies within him.
mn148:29-33.8: When touched by painful *vedanā*, he sorrows, grieves, laments, beats his breast, and becomes distraught.
mn148:29-33.9: The underlying tendency to aversion lies within him.
mn148:3.1: “The six internal dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood, the six external dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood, the six collections of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] are to be understood, the six collections of contact are to be understood, the six collections of *vedanā* are to be understood, the six collections of *taṇhā* are to be understood.
mn148:34.1: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition there arises a felt experience of *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn148:34.2: When touched by pleasant *vedanā*, he does not delight in it, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn148:34.3: The underlying tendency to passion does not lie within him.
mn148:34.4: When touched by painful *vedanā*, he does not sorrow, does not grieve, does not lament, does not beat his breast, and does not become distraught.
mn148:34.5: The underlying tendency to aversion does not lie within him.
mn148:34.6: When touched by neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands as it truly is the arising and vanishing of that *vedanā*, its gratification, danger, and escape.
mn148:34.7: The underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not lie within him.
mn148:34.8: That one, bhikkhus, having abandoned the underlying tendency to passion in regard to pleasant *vedanā*, having dispelled the underlying tendency to aversion in regard to painful *vedanā*, having uprooted the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] in regard to neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, having abandoned blindness [*avijjā*] and given rise to knowledge, would here and now bring dukkha to an end—this is possible.
mn148:35.1: Dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:36.1: Dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:37.1: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:38.1: Dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:39.1: “Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition there arises a felt experience of *sukha* or *dukkha* or neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha*.
mn148:39.2: When touched by pleasant *vedanā*, he does not delight in it, does not welcome it, and does not remain holding to it.
mn148:39.3: The underlying tendency to passion does not lie within him.
mn148:39.4: When touched by painful *vedanā*, he does not sorrow, does not grieve, does not lament, does not beat his breast, and does not become distraught.
mn148:39.5: The underlying tendency to aversion does not lie within him.
mn148:39.6: When touched by neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands as it truly is the arising and vanishing of that *vedanā*, its gratification, danger, and escape.
mn148:39.7: The underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] does not lie within him.
mn148:39.8: That one, bhikkhus, having abandoned the underlying tendency to passion in regard to pleasant *vedanā*, having dispelled the underlying tendency to aversion in regard to painful *vedanā*, having uprooted the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjā*] in regard to neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, having abandoned blindness [*avijjā*] and given rise to knowledge, would here and now bring dukkha to an end—this is possible.
mn148:4.1: ‘The six internal dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood’—
mn148:4.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:4.3: The eye-dimension [*cakkhāyatanaṁ*], the ear-dimension [*sotāyatanaṁ*], the nose-dimension [*ghānāyatanaṁ*], the tongue-dimension [*jivhāyatanaṁ*], the body-dimension [*kāyāyatanaṁ*], the mind-dimension [*manāyatanaṁ*].
mn148:4.4: ‘The six internal dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood’—
mn148:4.5: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:4.6: This is the first set of six.
mn148:40.1: Seeing thus, bhikkhus, the learned noble disciple becomes disenchanted with the eye, becomes disenchanted with *rūpas*, becomes disenchanted with eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*], becomes disenchanted with eye-contact, becomes disenchanted with *vedanā*, becomes disenchanted with *taṇhā*.
mn148:41.1: He becomes disenchanted with the ear, becomes disenchanted with sounds …
mn148:41.10: And while this explanation was being spoken, the minds of about sixty bhikkhus were liberated from the *āsavas* through non-clinging [*anupādāya*].
mn148:41.11: The Six Sets of Six is finished, the sixth.
mn148:41.2: becomes disenchanted with the nose, becomes disenchanted with smells …
mn148:41.3: becomes disenchanted with the tongue, becomes disenchanted with tastes …
mn148:41.4: becomes disenchanted with the body, becomes disenchanted with tangibles …
mn148:41.5: becomes disenchanted with the mind, becomes disenchanted with *dhammas*, becomes disenchanted with mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*], becomes disenchanted with mind-contact, becomes disenchanted with *vedanā*, becomes disenchanted with *taṇhā*.
mn148:41.6: Being disenchanted, he becomes dispassionate; through dispassion he is liberated. When liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn148:41.7: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’”
mn148:41.8: This is what the Bhagavā said.
mn148:41.9: Satisfied, those bhikkhus rejoiced in what the Bhagavā had said.
mn148:5.1: ‘The six external dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood’—
mn148:5.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:5.3: The *rūpa*-dimension [*rūpāyatanaṁ*], the sound-dimension [*saddāyatanaṁ*], the smell-dimension [*gandhāyatanaṁ*], the taste-dimension [*rasāyatanaṁ*], the tangible-dimension [*phoṭṭhabbāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of *dhammas* [*dhammāyatanaṁ*].
mn148:5.4: ‘The six external dimensions [*āyatanāni*] are to be understood’—
mn148:5.5: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:5.6: This is the second set of six.
mn148:6.1: ‘The six collections of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] are to be understood’—
mn148:6.10: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:6.11: This is the third set of six.
mn148:6.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:6.3: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises,
mn148:6.4: dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises,
mn148:6.5: dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises,
mn148:6.6: dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises,
mn148:6.7: dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises,
mn148:6.8: dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises.
mn148:6.9: ‘The six collections of consciousness [*viññāṇa*] are to be understood’—
mn148:7.1: ‘The six collections of contact are to be understood’—
mn148:7.10: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:7.11: This is the fourth set of six.
mn148:7.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:7.3: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.4: Dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.5: Dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.6: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.7: Dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.8: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact.
mn148:7.9: ‘The six collections of contact are to be understood’—
mn148:8.1: ‘The six collections of *vedanā* are to be understood’—
mn148:8.10: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:8.11: This is the fifth set of six.
mn148:8.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:8.3: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.4: Dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.5: Dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.6: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.7: Dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.8: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*.
mn148:8.9: ‘The six collections of *vedanā* are to be understood’—
mn148:9.1: ‘The six collections of *taṇhā* are to be understood’—
mn148:9.10: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn148:9.11: This is the sixth set of six.
mn148:9.2: so it was said. And in dependence on what was this said?
mn148:9.3: Dependent on the eye and *rūpas*, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*; with *vedanā* as condition, *taṇhā*.
mn148:9.4: Dependent on the ear and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:9.5: Dependent on the nose and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:9.6: Dependent on the tongue and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:9.7: Dependent on the body and tangibles, body-consciousness [*kāyviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn148:9.8: Dependent on the mind and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises; the coming together of the three is contact; with contact as condition, *vedanā*; with *vedanā* as condition, *taṇhā*.
mn148:9.9: ‘The six collections of *taṇhā* are to be understood’—
